summary: Image B is more visually stimulating, triggering stronger reactions to colors, sharp edges, and written meaning, though it may require more mental effort to process. Image A is more effective at helping viewers recognize familiar objects or faces.

detailed.winner_reason: The visual infographic activates broader brain regions for processing, creating a richer and more engaging cognitive experience than text alone.
detailed.emotional_impact: The infographic creates a sense of discovery and engagement through its visual variety. The text-heavy design triggers the reading circuits but misses the emotional and spatial processing that makes content feel alive.
detailed.visual_attention: The infographic distributes attention across multiple brain regions simultaneously, creating a richer neural experience. Text-heavy content funnels attention narrowly through the lingual gyrus reading pathway.
detailed.memory_retention: Visual information is retained 6x longer than text. The infographic activates both visual and semantic memory systems, encoding the information through multiple pathways for better recall.
recommendations: Convert key data points into visual elements like charts, icons, or progress bars
Break long text blocks into scannable visual sections with clear hierarchy
Use color coding to create visual categories that the brain can process faster than reading labels
